topic: Analemma figure-8 loop ratio 2.66 — c7 verification of c6 minor patch (ISS-2188 area-to-perimeter bullet "0.4% tolerance implied below" clause dropped)
change_summary: Decider commit cb1a9c3 patched ISS-2188 by removing "1.9% still exceeds the 0.4% tolerance implied below" residual from the area-to-perimeter bullet. New bullet text: "area-to-perimeter ratio ~ 2.61 (deviation from 2.66: -1.9%, but this is a derived rather than a primitive metric)". The 0.4%/0.2% tolerance discrepancy is fully eliminated.
our_argument_summary: WIN-019 (Analemma figure-8 loop ratio 2.66) remains Misleading. The three-pronged falsification (undefined metric, no candidate ratio matches at quoted precision, 2.66 reproduces 8/3 gear ratio to 0.25%) is intact. The c6->c7 patch eliminated the last internal-tolerance inconsistency by dropping the trailing parenthetical from the area-to-perimeter bullet. All five enumerated bullets now follow a single uniform precision claim (±0.2% half-width from the dome's own three-sig-fig quotation) with no competing tolerance values appearing anywhere in the paragraph. The bullet now correctly notes the area-to-perimeter metric is derived rather than primitive — which is the substantive point and the only reason area-to-perimeter is mentioned at all (completeness of enumeration).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: monitor.py has no analemma domain — no fetch, no comparison, no validation. The 2.66 value is a static string with data_source="Spirograph" indicating it is a gear ratio (8/3 = 2.66667), not a measurement.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None — there is no comparison logic to escape from.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — code_analysis unchanged by c6->c7 patch.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Carries forward from c6. monitor.py contains no analemma domain. Value lives only as static string in inject_ai_layer.py confirmed_wins with data_source="Spirograph". Repository snapshot verified in prior cycles.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Carries forward from c6. Standard physics derives the analemma figure-8 from obliquity (23.44 deg) and eccentricity (0.0167); asymmetry is real and documented (Meeus 1998; Holbrow 2013). Standard physics does not define "loop ratio". The dome asserts a new ratio that does not match any standard one — not a relabeling, an assertion.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Carries forward from c6. Asymmetry of the analemma characterized since 18th century (Meeus 1998 ch. 28). Dome value 2.66 is the Spirograph gear ratio 8/3 = 2.66667 with data_source="Spirograph"; nothing in the pipeline compares it to a sky observation.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Carries forward from c6. No derivation from disc_radius (20,015 km), firmament_height (9,086 km), or sun_altitude (5,733 km) to 2.66 exists. The value is a free gear-ratio parameter inside the Spirograph mechanism.

kernel_of_truth.description: Unchanged from c6. The dome correctly observes that the real analemma is asymmetric — the southern (winter) lobe IS larger than the northern (summer) lobe, and this asymmetry has a well-defined numerical magnitude depending on which ratio you choose (data/wins.json:WIN-019.detail_evidence enumerates five: height 2.25, width 3.01, area 6.69, perimeter 2.56, area-to-perimeter 2.61). The dome correctly identifies that something quantitative is there to be measured.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The specific value 2.66 does not match any of the five primitive ratios at the dome's own quoted precision (closest is perimeter at 2.56, missing by 3.8% — an order of magnitude above the dome's implied ±0.2% from three-sig-fig precision). 2.66 reproduces 8/3 = 2.66667 to 0.25%, with data_source="Spirograph" labelling the source as the gear mechanism itself (data/wins.json:WIN-019.detail_evidence). The 8/3 gear in a hypocycloid produces an 8-cusped curve, not a figure-8 — so the named mechanism cannot even generate the curve it claims to predict (monitor/analyst/globe-fingerprints/WIN-019.json spirograph_analysis).

deeper_analysis: Advocate-mode check post-patch: (1) Defender attack "your bullets list five different ratios — you are cherry-picking". Reply: the five ratios are an exhaustive enumeration of all standard analemma geometric ratios (height, width, area, perimeter, area-to-perimeter); listing them and showing none matches 2.66 at quoted precision is the opposite of cherry-picking. (2) Defender attack "you complain about quoted precision but apply your own tolerance". Reply: the +-0.2% half-width is derived from the dome's own three-sig-fig quotation "2.66" -- standard sig-fig convention is what assigns the tolerance, not us. (3) Defender attack "the patched bullet still calls area-to-perimeter a derived metric — what about the gear ratio interpretation?" Reply: the gear-ratio is identified separately as 8/3 gear, NOT as a fifth analemma ratio. The bullet's "derived rather than primitive" is about the math relationship (area-to-perimeter is a composite of two primitives), not about sky-vs-gear provenance. Cross-WIN consistency: WIN-019 sits in the same Spirograph-derived cluster as WIN-018 (vertical analemma) and WIN-026 (horizontal analemma offset) — same code mechanism, same verdict pattern. The 8/3 -> 8-cusped curve mapping in globe-fingerprints/WIN-019.json is referenced inline and consistent. Quantitative spot-check: 8/3 = 2.66667; |2.66 - 2.66667| / 2.66667 = 0.25%, matches text. Perimeter ratio 2.56; |2.56 - 2.66| / 2.66 = 3.8%, matches text. Math is sound.

citations_unchecked: Lynch 2012 Irish Math. Soc. Bulletin 69 pp. 47-56 (not re-verified this cycle)

summary_for_decider: BATCH VERIFICATION qid=400 (WIN-019 c7, batch position 3). c6 ISS-2188 patch landed cleanly: "0.4% tolerance implied below" residual removed from area-to-perimeter bullet. Internal precision consistency now uniform at +-0.2% throughout the paragraph (main sentence and "must agree to within +-0.2%" threshold; all five bullets use percentage deviations without competing tolerance claims). No new holes. Verdict Misleading continues to hold. No carry-over.
